summary: Not many books have been published on the Issei, especially in English. We're trying to collect and share their experiences with the Sansei and Yonsei generations. The Issei were the first generation of Japanese immigrants, and their stories are largely unknown even in Japan. My family, including my older brother Chojiro, was part of that pioneering group establishing communities and churches in the U.S. around the early 1900s.

The Sansei are the third generation of Japanese Americans, and we want to make sure they understand the history and struggles of the Issei. When I was involved in the Buddhist Church, I noticed that the Nisei and Sansei often found it hard to connect with traditional Buddhism because it was taught in Japanese. We tried to engage younger generations through activities like sports to keep them involved. Sharing our history with the Sansei is important to keep our culture alive.

The Yonsei, or fourth generation Japanese Americans, are also part of our efforts to pass down the Issei's history. We want them to learn about the early immigrants' experiences and the challenges faced by their ancestors. Our historical records and church histories are being compiled to educate both the Sansei and Yonsei. This way, the legacy of the Issei continues through the generations.

When I first came to the U.S. as a student, I attended the Methodist Church in Los Angeles, led by Minister Yamaha. He was very good at guiding young people like me. Although I came from a Buddhist family, I visited the Methodist Church on Sundays while attending school during the week. It was interesting to hear people share their experiences and feelings about being protected by God. I also attended other churches like a Baptist Church during that time.

Los Angeles was where I first connected with the Methodist Church under Minister Yamaha's leadership. It was a place where many young Japanese immigrants gathered, and I visited the church there on Sundays while attending school. Later, Los Angeles became one of the locations where our Buddhist Church had a branch. It was part of the broader Japanese immigrant community in California.

I was born in Hiroshima in 1901, a place where Buddhism was widely practiced. My father was an ardent Buddhist and came to the U.S. first in 1894, scouting for young people to immigrate. He sent over five hundred young people from Hiroshima to the U.S., which explains why so many immigrants came from that area. Our family has a long history there, with my father representing the 42nd generation and me the 43rd.

Seattle was an important city for our family and the Buddhist Church. My older brother Chojiro stayed in Seattle to take care of young Japanese immigrants. There were seven Buddhist churches in Seattle at one time, though only three are known today. Our family was deeply involved in the church's establishment and activities there, and I have many records about the church's history in Seattle.

San Francisco was the site of a significant celebration marking fifty years since the Buddhist Church was started in the U.S. I planned to attend this event and contribute to the memorial project by compiling and publishing the church history. The city has been a central location for the Buddhist community and its branches. It holds a special place in our efforts to preserve our religious and cultural heritage.

Chojiro Fujii was my older brother and the first president of our Buddhist Church, established in 1901 when he was just twenty-three. He worked hard to establish the church and took care of young Japanese immigrants in Seattle. He was also involved in our family’s hotel business, which was the first Japanese hotel in Seattle. His leadership laid the foundation for our community's religious and social life in the U.S.

I am Yoshito Fujii, born in Hiroshima in 1901, and I have been deeply involved in preserving the history of the Buddhist Church in America. I attended the University of Washington, majoring in Sociology, and have kept extensive records of our church's activities and membership since the 1920s. Alongside my brothers, I helped compile the church history and served as president and adviser. My work has been crucial in documenting our community's legacy for future generations.

Hisato Fujii is my brother and one of the successive presidents of our Buddhist Church. Like Chojiro and me, he played an important role in the church's leadership and activities. Our family has been central to the church's establishment and growth in the U.S. His contributions are part of the rich history we are preserving and sharing with the community.

I attended the University of Washington, majoring in Sociology, and during my studies, I found old records from 1925 about the Buddhist Church. The university library has expressed interest in our community's records, especially those related to the relocation camps during the war in Minnesota. I plan to donate these valuable documents to the university to preserve our history. The university has been a key place for research and archiving our community's experiences.

Our Buddhist Church is part of the Jodo Shinshu tradition, and I have worked closely with church officials to verify and notarize our historical records. The Jodo Shinshu organization has branches in places like Sacramento, Fresno, Los Angeles, and San Jose. This tradition has been central to our religious life and community cohesion in the U.S. for over fifty years.

I sent our church's historical records to Mr. Hirota of Nishi Honganji Jr., who approved and notarized them. Nishi Honganji is a significant organization within our Buddhist tradition, and their recognition has been important for validating our community's history. This connection helps maintain ties between our American church and the broader Buddhist world in Japan.

Sacramento is one of the five branches of our Buddhist Church in California, along with Fresno, Los Angeles, San Jose, and our local church. Our church's history includes the establishment and growth of these branches to serve Japanese American communities throughout the state. Sacramento's branch plays a vital role in maintaining our religious and cultural traditions.

Fresno is another important branch of our Buddhist Church in California. Along with Sacramento, Los Angeles, San Jose, and our local church, Fresno helps support the spiritual needs of Japanese Americans in the region. Our church history records include details about these branches and their activities over the decades.

San Jose is one of the five branches of our Buddhist Church in California, alongside Sacramento, Fresno, Los Angeles, and our local church. These branches have been essential in fostering community and religious life among Japanese Americans. Our historical records document the establishment and leadership of these churches.

During World War II, many Japanese Americans, including members of our community, were relocated to camps in places like Minnesota. I have extensive records about the relocation camp in Minnesota, filling three volumes with detailed information. The University of Washington library is very interested in these documents, and I plan to donate them to preserve this important part of our history.

I came to the United States right after World War I ended, in 1919. The war broke out in 1914, and by the time it was over, I had graduated junior high school in Japan and decided to pursue higher education in the U.S. This period was a turning point for many Japanese immigrants seeking new opportunities abroad.

My father first came to the U.S. in 1894, during the 27th year of the Meiji period. This was a time when Japan was modernizing and many young people were encouraged to go abroad. My father stayed for two years scouting the northern U.S. with my brother Chojiro, and then returned to Japan to encourage others to emigrate. This period shaped the early wave of Japanese immigration to America.

I come from a Buddhist family in Hiroshima where Buddhism was widely practiced. My father was a devoted Buddhist and believed strongly in 'namuamidabutsu,' which he taught me to say before meals. Buddhism was central to our family and community life, especially through the Jodo Shinshu tradition. However, I noticed that younger generations like the Nisei and Sansei found it difficult to understand because it was taught in Japanese, so we tried to adapt by including activities like sports.

During my early years in the U.S., I attended Broadway High School. It was part of my educational journey after coming here as a student. My time there helped me integrate into American society while maintaining my cultural roots. I also balanced my school life with church activities and community involvement.

I also attended Franklin High School in Seattle during my youth. This was part of my educational path after arriving from Japan. The school experience was important as I navigated life between two cultures. It complemented my involvement in church and community activities in Seattle.
